data.title: ChargePoint Q4: The Misery Continues
data.description:
After the bell on Wednesday, we received fiscal fourth quarter results from ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. (CHPT) for its January-ending period. The charging company has consistently failed to meet long-term revenue growth estimates, sending shares sharply lower over time. The company did end up beating Q4 top-line expectations and improving its balance sheet during the quarter. However, a very soft revenue forecast and some significantly favorable working capital changes diminished what could have been meaningful progress.

Previous Coverage Of The Name

The last time I covered ChargePoint was back in December, right after the company's fiscal Q3 report. That was one of the company's better reports in some time, as there wasn't a lot of major bad news announced. While things looked like they may be about to turn around, investors haven't exactly bought in since, with the stock down more than 37% to Wednesday's close, while the S&P 500 was basically flat over that time.

A Look At The Q4 Report

For the final quarter of its fiscal year 2026, ChargePoint announced revenues of $109.3 million. This number was up 7% over the prior year period, and it beat street estimates by more than $4.4 million. The company saw more than 9.5% growth in its Network Charging Systems segment, while Subscription revenue was up almost 11%. Other revenues took a more than 16% tumble. This was the company's 5th revenue beat in the last 6 quarters, although a number of those beats have come due to significantly reduced street estimates.

ChargePoint saw a 3% gross margin improvement on both a GAAP and non-GAAP basis over the prior year period, mainly due to higher margin subscription revenues becoming a larger portion of the revenue total. However, operating expenses were also up, so despite the revenue jump, the operating loss didn't even decline by $2 million, coming in at nearly $53 million for the quarter. The net loss showed a bit more improvement, but that was mainly a function of the gain due to the debt exchange I previously covered.

Unfortunately for investors, management did not paint a rosy picture about the current period. Revenues for the April-ending period are expected to be in a range of $90 million to $100 million. Even the high end of that forecast was quite a bit below the $105.53 million the street was looking for. As the chart below shows, fiscal 2027 estimates have been coming down for some time, and this guidance likely won't stop that bleeding.

Balance Sheet And Cash Flow Update

There is some seasonality in this business, so despite the large losses, Q4 cash burn was only a couple of million, and the figure did improve year over year. ChargePoint did bring its total amount of debt on the balance sheet down by nearly $85 million in the quarter. Thus, the net debt amount improved quite a bit during the quarter, as seen below. Cash burn improved significantly over the prior year period but still was $67 million for the full year.

The company also saw some major benefits from working capital items in the quarter, as its accounts receivable declined a bit and payables rose, part of which was due to seasonality. Total working capital on the balance sheet, which is current assets minus current liabilities, declined sequentially from over $190 million to less than $77 million. Given the weak revenue forecast and Q1 generally seeing some seasonal cash burn, I wouldn't be surprised to see management raise some capital here.

The Current Valuation Picture

As of Wednesday's close, ChargePoint was going for about 0.35 times its currently expected revenues for this year. That is a huge discount compared to fellow charging name EVgo, Inc. (EVGO), which goes for about 0.76 times its respective fiscal year's (December 2026 ending) expected sales. EVgo is expected to show more than 17% revenue growth in its current fiscal year and another nearly 27% in 2027. On the flip side, ChargePoint was estimated to show less than 9% growth in the current fiscal year going into Wednesday's report, with a little more than 15% in its next fiscal year.

Despite these ongoing troubles, the street has remained moderately bullish on ChargePoint shares, with the average price target of $10.19 going into the Q4 report representing more than 56% upside for the stock, although that average is down $1.50 since my previous article. This is despite two total sell ratings against just one buy, with seven analysts having holds. Also, analysts have seemingly been playing catch-up with the decline in shares over time. Just a year ago, the average target was over $41, split-adjusted, and about five years ago it topped out at $1,000 per share.

Final Thoughts And Recommendation

In the end, ChargePoint disappointed investors with its current quarter revenue forecast on Wednesday, sending shares tumbling in the after-hours session. While fiscal Q4 revenues did beat, it didn't result in major operating loss reductions, with a GAAP operating margin of roughly negative 51% on a full-year basis. While the debt deal helped, the balance sheet is still in poor shape, and that's despite some major seasonal working capital benefits.

As revenue estimates are likely to take another leg lower, I have to keep a "Sell" rating on the stock here today. The company isn't getting the top line to grow enough to significantly improve its income statement, and cash burn remains a major problem. With another capital raise likely coming and a current U.S. government that's not a big fan of electric vehicles, the stock could easily set more new lows this year.

data.title: Shopify: Why It Could Double From Here
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) is down 28% YTD as the stock has been clubbed with all generic software in the 2026 SaaSpocalypse meltdown.

Despite the company reporting strong Q4 FY25 earnings, with management projecting accelerating revenue growth in Q1, investors chose to solely focus on the company’s free cash flow margin, which contracted on a year-over-year basis and is expected to further shrink from current levels next quarter.

This is the case as management is heavily investing in their Agentic Commerce roadmap, as they are positioning themselves as a unified operating system for commerce. As Shay Boloor from Futurm Equities points out, “Shopify sits underneath checkout, payments, fulfillment logic, and merchant operations, and these rails do not go away just because the front-end interface changes. Instead, it should expand Shopify’s surface area because more ways to shop mean more ways for merchants to transact through the Shopify platform.”

That is exactly why Shopify announced UCP (Universal Commerce Protocol) that they have co-developed with Google (NASDAQ:GOOG), which is an open standard for any agent to connect with any brand on the Internet, while Shopify still powers the whole experience for both buyers and merchants.

With forward revenue growth estimates revised higher from here, I believe that investor skepticism on the company’s strategy to sacrifice short-term free cash flow margins is misplaced. Especially when its operating leverage is expanding.

The way I see it, the stock can easily double from here to $240+ per share once sentiment improves along with a stable economy. As a result, I will continue to reiterate my “buy” rating on the stock.

Strong Revenue Print Across Merchant Solutions & Subscriptions

Shopify reported its Q4 FY25 earnings last month, where it continued to grow its revenues at a steady pace of 31% YoY to $3.67B. This brings the FY25 revenue growth rate to 30%, 400 basis points higher than FY24.

Out of the $3.67B in revenue, Merchant Solutions grew at 35% YoY, contributing close to 79% of revenue (up from 75% in Q3), with GMV (Gross Merchandise Value) growing 31% YoY, driven both by adding new merchants across sizes and expanding within existing cohorts. At the same time, the penetration of Shopify Payments also continued to deepen, growing 300 basis points sequentially to 68% of total GMV.

Meanwhile, Subscription revenue grew at approximately 17% YoY, driven by a larger percentage of subscriptions coming from higher-priced plans across both existing merchants and new merchants joining the platform and higher variable platform fees.

In terms of their momentum across their growth levers, their International revenue now makes up over 32% of their total revenue mix, growing at a faster pace than their US markets at 36% YoY, as they welcomed several global brands onto the platform.

Meanwhile, their Offline channel revenue made up over 6.4% of total revenue in FY25, with GMV growing 29% YoY during Q4. Simultaneously, their B2B Commerce is also showing tremendous strength, with GMV from B2B merchants up 84% in Q4 and 96% in 2025. As management pointed out during the earnings call, "These channels continue to be important growth areas for Shopify, expanding access points into our ecosystem and broadening our addressable market to include more businesses and industries.”

Shopify Not Just An App—It Is The Operating System For Agentic Commerce

Since the SaaSpocalypse meltdown started in 2026, Shopify too has been clubbed along with all software companies, with shares now down 28% YTD.

Like I have now explained in several of my posts, the idea behind the SaaSpocalypse is tied to how Agentic AI is rewriting the software tech stack, where the UI/UX layer is increasingly at risk of getting abstracted away if AI agents can autonomously run workflows without a human being ever logging into the software tool, thus disrupting the predictable “seat-based” pricing model.

In this case, the fear is that if people start shopping through AI agents, chat interfaces, or new discovery surfaces, Shopify will get disrupted.

But here is the thing.

Shopify is not just an app but is instead an operating system for commerce. As Shay Boloor from Futurm Equities points out, Shopify sits underneath checkout, payments, fulfillment logic, and merchant operations, and these rails do not go away just because the front-end interface changes. Instead, it should expand Shopify’s surface area because more ways to shop means more ways for merchants to transact through the Shopify platform.

That is exactly why Shopify announced UCP (Universal Commerce Protocol) that they have co-developed with Google, which is an open standard for any agent to connect with any brand on the Internet. This is how the management described it below.

“It's payment agnostic by design. It keeps the merchants essential checkout logic intact without forcing them to rebuild their customizations over and over again to fit our system. UCP is the only protocol that covers the full commerce journey end-to-end from search to cart, then checkout to post-order, and it's already being used by the world's leading retailers. Put simply, Shopify is foundational in powering the commerce layer of the AI era, and we're just getting started.”

In all of this, Shopify will still power the whole experience for both buyers and merchants, which means that things like order processing and payments will run through Shopify Payments in the back end, as they help merchants sell across a new surface while simplifying the complexity through their retail operating system.

In addition to the above, Shopify also launched Agentic Storefronts which will enable merchants to syndicate their products through their Catalog offering to major AI platforms like Google AI Mode, Gemini, and ChatGPT, allowing them access to “millions of potential buyers." Shopify simultaneously launched Agentic Plan that is geared towards merchants who are even on the Shopify platform to allow them to sell their products as well, thus increasing their surface area even more.

Meanwhile, their on-platform AI assistant Sidekick has also been doing some heavy lifting in proactively helping merchants grow their business and operate smarter, where

Agentic plan, which is for merchants who are not even on Shopify to allow them to syndicate their products as well, which allows us to begin a relationship with merchants, again, that are not currently on Shopify. Here’s an example that management shared during the earnings call that demonstrates how it is demonstrating product and conversion data to drive superior business results for merchants.

“Last week, Sidekick Pulse made a recommendation to one of our jewelry brands. It suggested bundling 4 separate products and selling them together as a stack. Why? Because it knew that those 4 products were already best sellers, and it also knew that bundles tend to convert better and drive up cart value. Personalized data analysis paired with intelligence gained from hundreds of millions of other transactions. This is where our AI assistant really becomes the AI co-founder. It's bespoke, it's intuitive, and it's unmistakably Shopify.”

Investors Remain Skeptical On Free Cash Flow Margin Squeeze

This brings me to the overall profitability metrics. Starting with gross profit, it grew 25% YoY with a margin contraction of 200 basis points. This is the case as the revenue mix shifted more towards payments, which carry lower gross margins than Subscriptions, but scale much more efficiently from an operating expense standpoint.

This is exactly what we are seeing with operating expenses as a percentage of revenue declining both on a year-over-year and sequential basis to 29%. For the full year FY25, operating expenses as a percentage of revenue came in at 35%, which marked a 300 basis point improvement over FY24 levels, as they drive headcount efficiency across R&D, sales and marketing, and G&A.

Not only that, the strength in their revenue cohorts has also been growing over time, as you can see below, which further explains their expanding operating leverage.

However, what investors did not like in the company’s earnings was free cash flow margin, which shrunk 300 basis points on a year-over-year basis to 19%, while management guided for free cash flow margin to shrink even further in Q1 to the low-to-mid teens range. During the earnings call, management explained that in order to support their long-term vision, they would continue to need to invest in laying the groundwork for Agentic commerce rather than pursue higher free cash flow margins in the near term.

Despite management projecting a faster-than-expected revenue growth in Q1 that would mark an acceleration both on a year-over-year and sequential basis, investors remained skeptical.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, consensus estimates have revised their revenue growth projections for the company higher, as can be seen below. As of now, the company is expected to grow in the mid- to high-twenties range over the next three years to generate $22.83B in revenue by FY28, which underscores their superior positioning as the operating system for Agentic Commerce.

From a profitability standpoint, on the other hand, analysts have revised their growth expectations lower, which could be a function of their apprehension towards ongoing pressures on free cash flow margin from the company’s investments for the future.

The stock is currently trading at a forward PE ratio of 66 based on its FY26 earnings per share, which is not particularly cheap. However, the PE ratio compresses to roughly 36x when taking FY28 earnings per share into account. This is roughly 2x that of the 10-year average S&P 500 price-to-earnings multiple of 18.8.

However, given that its earnings per share are growing at an average rate of 36% every year over the next three years, this is roughly 4x that of the average S&P 500 company that grows their earnings by 9% annually.

In other words, Shopify should therefore fetch a price-to-earnings multiple of at least 72, which, if applied to its projected FY28 earnings per share, will translate to a price target of $244 per share, representing an upside of 100%+ over a 3-year investment horizon.

Risks And Conclusions

Although the stock presents a very attractive upside potential of doubling from here, it may continue to remain under pressure in the short term at least if investor sentiment towards companies sacrificing their free cash margins to support long-term growth does not improve.

At the same time, the state of the global economy and consumer spending are also important drivers for the stock as they directly impact GMV. Although Shopify has successfully diversified 32% of its business revenue outside of the US, any form of macroeconomic slowdown will hurt the stock in the short term.

Keeping these above risks in mind, I still believe that the risk-reward looks extremely attractive at current levels that compensate for the potential downside risks as long as investors exercise appropriate risk management.

As a result, I will reiterate my “buy” rating on Shopify with an updated price target of $244 per share.

data.title: SPXX: Set To Become Larger With DIAX And BXMX Merge
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Nuveen S&P 500 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (SPXX) is set to get larger after the management team from Nuveen proposed a merger into the fund. The acquired funds are set to be the Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund (BXMX) and Nuveen Dow 30 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (DIAX). These two are funds we previously touched on, and for investors in BXMX and DIAX, there will be some changes, but the general concept of the overall strategy won't be shifting too much. In essence, investors will still own a call writing fund, but the focus of the overwrite and the level of the overwrite will be changing.

A key metric for closed-end funds always comes down to a discount/premium inherent in the fund structure. This is no different with the merger, as the discounts of the funds should start to converge. So far, SPXX is trading at a slightly narrower discount relative to DIAX and BXMX, making the merger, if it took place today, slightly positive for those investors. Historically, SPXX has been the name that has generally tended to trade either at the narrowest discount or the highest premium.

As the mergers are still pending shareholder approval, there could be some opportunity for investors to switch into DIAX ahead of what is generally a likely approval. It isn't too uncommon for closed-end funds to see delays in getting these proposals approved.

On its own, SPXX is trading at a relatively attractive discount, making it look like a compelling investment option to consider in today's market.

SPXX Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.22
  • Discount/Premium: -8.53%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.63%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.91%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $347.8 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

SPXX's investment objective and brief strategy to achieve that is: "attractive total return with less volatility than the S&P 500 Index by investing in a U.S. equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movement of the S&P 500 Index, as well as selling call options on 35-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns."

They note that the longer-term average is to overwrite at around 55%. At the latest reporting, the fund's average call option percentage comes in at 60%, with the weighted average days to expiration of nearly 18 days.

One of the more interesting notes to perhaps mention is that SPXX is actually the smallest of the three funds, with BXMX being significantly larger at nearly $1.7 billion AUM. DIAX is also not too insignificant at around $627 million AUM. None of the funds are leveraged through borrowings, just an overwrite strategy utilizing writing call options. With that, if the merger takes place, the total AUM for the new SPXX would become a hefty ~$2.67 billion fund. That is quite meaningful in the closed-end fund space.

Strategy Differences

Despite the size of the fund that should provide relatively better overall liquidity for investors, as is a common tout of positive CEF merger outcomes, investors are likely more concerned with what they may be owning going forward.

As we noted above, SPXX focuses on around 55% overwrite on the S&P 500 Index. They write call options on the index rather than individual holdings, but then have a portfolio tilted with a heavy emphasis to mirror the index itself.

This is done with 344 holdings relative to the indexes ~500 positions, but the overall sector allocation is still highly overlapping with the major index itself. That's an index that has become predominantly allocated to the technology sector in a hefty weighting, which is reflective of the top holdings with names like NVIDIA (NVDA) commanding an over 8% allocation, with it being over 7% of the SPDR S&P 500 Index ETF (SPY) as well.

BXMX

BXMX is quite similar in its approach, but the key difference is that the fund targets a 100% overwrite of its portfolio.

BXMX's investment objective is to "seek attractive total return with less volatility than the S&P 500 Index..." They intend to achieve this through "...investing in a global equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the S&P 500 Index and by selling index call options covering approximately 100% of the Fund's equity portfolio value with a goal of enhancing the portfolio's risk-adjusted returns."

With their latest reporting showing an average call option percentage of 99%, they are right near their target. However, they also have the weighted average days of expiration at ~38, more than double that of SPXX. What that can do is bring in larger premiums upfront to help fund distributions, but it also means less tweaking over time. The result is that the fund could be running into its upside cap, a common downfall of call-writing funds, more regularly.

Also noteworthy is that the fund holds 215 positions, significantly fewer than SPXX. At the same time, the same holdings are strong representations of the S&P 500 Index, and therefore, mirroring SPXX.

The sector allocation places slightly more weight on technology but is otherwise going to be very similar.

DIAX

In terms of DIAX, it may be the investors who would see the biggest change if or when the merger with SPXX takes place.

DIAX's investment objective is "to offer regular distributions through a strategy that seeks attractive total returns with less volatility than the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA or "Dow 30")..." In an attempt to achieve this objective, the fund will invest "in an equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the DJIA, as well as selling call options on 35-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio (with a 55% long-term target) in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns."

As of their last report, the fund was overwritten by 60%, and the average days to expiration are similar to SPXX, also at around 18 days. What might be interesting to note is that despite having a DJIA mention in the name and noted in the investment strategy, the fund actually writes call options against the S&P 500 Index still.

That is despite the fund's overall individual underlying portfolio allocation placing more emphasis on the value-oriented sectors, similar to the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Though they note 38 total holdings at the end of Jan. 31, 2026.

The sector allocation can be seen below, which shows us that financials are the highest allocation.

With that, it is an interesting mismatch that they employ from their underlying portfolio relative to the overwrite strategy they employ on top of that.

When it comes to historical returns over the long run, SPXX comes out on top—which is something that we shouldn't see as overall too surprising.

SPXX overwrites less of its portfolio relative to BXMX, and in what has been a rising market for much of the last decade and, in particular, the last few years, that would easily see SPXX outperform.

DIAX also has that mismatch portfolio vs. call writing strategy. That means its underlying portfolio can underperform relative to the tech-heavy SPY that has been leading the way higher. On the flip side of that, the fund's call writing strategy then runs into the upside cap more, and when they have to close or roll their trades, they still experience those potential losses being generated on that side as well.

Where DIAX's strategy should work the best is in the current 2026 environment. That is something we would have seen with the Parametric Equity Premium Income ETF (PAPI). An ETF that has a similar mismatch portfolio, but it is absolutely destroying even DIAX this year. Tech down and value up means that not only is the fund experiencing strong underlying portfolio gains, but the premiums being collected are also producing enhanced returns. This is because they are pocketing much of the premiums when they go to roll the position as the S&P 500 Index declines or moves sideways.

Distribution

Finally, it is also worth noting that investors will still be receiving a quarterly distribution if the merger goes through and DIAX and BXMX investors become SPXX investors.

At the current rate, that works out to 7.63% and a NAV rate of 6.98%. DIAX is currently at 7.94%, with a NAV rate of 6.98%—while BXMX investors are at 7.5% at a NAV rate of 6.72%.

All these funds require capital gains to fund their payouts, and that can come from the premiums received or the underlying portfolio.

So on that front, not much is changing at all. The actual distribution rates are similar enough, and the way they get covered will be the same.

Conclusion

Investors in BXMX and DIAX are being presented with an opportunity to become shareholders of SPXX. Based on the current valuation, this is favorable for DIAX investors and slightly favorable for BXMX investors. Despite SPXX being the smaller fund, it is being proposed as the acquirer. This is likely because of its longer-term track record of better total returns, with its greater overwrite flexibility being a key reason for that. Additionally, the discount is narrower and has been relatively narrower for that name as well, which also makes it a better candidate for investors. If I were a shareholder, I would vote to approve the merger.

data.title: CrowdStrike Earnings: Another Blow To AI-As-A-Threat Narrative
data.description:
CrowdStrike: What Worked And What Could Be Next

CrowdStrike (CRWD) ranks among those companies that quickly moved from startup status to dominance. After all, the company started in 2011 and now has a market cap of almost $100B. Its success is based on understanding a flaw in the cybersecurity market. Traditional antivirus software worked reactively, meaning that it relied on a library of known threats. Once a new threat was identified, a new update was sent to all the users. CrowdStrike, on the other hand, is cloud-native and developed its Falcon platform so that any change or update is instantly deployed to all the endpoints across the globe. This creates a flywheel effect, which allows the company to immediately use any single attack blocked on one laptop or endpoint to be used to train and develop a new protection for all of CrowdStrike's customers. So, the company is one of the winners of the cloud economy, where platforms achieve success also because they reach the scale to become dominant, thanks to the benefits of network effects. This has also enabled the company to cross-sell many modules, starting from endpoint security and then rapidly adding other modules, such as cloud security, identity protection, and similar. Like most cloud-based businesses, CrowdStrike uses a SaaS business model to earn its revenues. In particular, CrowdStrike explains that it is superior to other cloud-based cybersecurity companies (read "Palo Alto Networks") because it has a single console-single agent architecture instead of a fragmented one. This means that CrowdStrike's Single Agent can turn on and off the modules a customer wants, without needing to install any new agents. As a result, the so-called "agent fatigue" is reduced for internal IT teams. This is particularly useful on the Falcon Flex Module, which was recently launched to allow companies to have consumption-based plans. The outcome of this advantage is that, when we deal with CrowdStrike, seeing its ARR grow doesn't only mean that the company is going to generate more revenues down the line, but that it is widening its reach and increasing the impact of its network effects, thus strengthening its structural moat. This is how platform economics work.

In July 2024, CrowdStrike made it to the headlines because of a global outage. The stock tumbled, although it quickly recovered, and investors realized how embedded CrowdStrike had become in the cybersecurity space. In hindsight, we can consider the 2024 outage as the stress-test event that showed the level of success CrowdStrike has achieved in a little more than a decade.

With this in mind, the narrative around the stock has, however, been darkened by the appearance of AI security tools, which could represent a menace to CrowdStrike's business. This is why the stock has been trending downward since its peak in mid-November 2025. In particular, Claude Code Security, launched in late February 2026, seems to be able to scan codebases and find vulnerabilities that had never been detected (the news talked about over 500). If you missed it, CRWD tumbled 8%.

The reaction by CRWD CEO George Kurtz went viral. He published on X the answer that Claude itself gave him when he asked it to build a CrowdStrike replacement. Claude said that it is something that "it is an infrastructure product". The idea is that Claude competes more with analysis tools (Snyk, Checkmarx, JFrog, and similar) rather than with platforms and ecosystems. This means that it could actually be implemented within a platform to make it even better. In technical jargon, Claude Code Security is a pre-deployment tool, while CRWD's Falcon is a post-deployment platform. So, we need to frame the competitive threat correctly.

Actually, the real threat could be that AI reduces the attack surface, which could lead some companies to reduce their security budget. However, AI will also be used to create new threats, so I don't think that cybersecurity budgets will be reduced. If AI becomes an accelerator of security attacks, then CRWD becomes a key player to provide sophisticated threat detection and solutions. It is what CRWD wants to do with its Charlotte AI.

I have taken the stance that, generally speaking, the sell-off in SaaS companies is overdone. There will be companies that could be forced out of business by AI, but they are not the strongest ones, which will actually benefit from integrating AI within their ecosystems. The real danger could come from a re-rating and multiple compression, but we will get to that in a minute, once we review CrowdStrike's earnings.

CrowdStrike's Q4 FY26 Earnings Review

CrowdStrike beat top and bottom line expectations, reporting quarterly revenue growth of 23% to $1.31B (consensus: $1.3B), and non-GAAP EPS of $1.12 (consensus: $1.10).

When we deal with this kind of company, we have to keep in mind that the main drivers of the stock are metrics, such as the ending ARR, the net new ARR, the net retention rate, the operating margin, and FCF. Of course, the guidance is also important. EPS, on the other hand, matters less because we are talking about fast-growing companies that are still not totally focused on profits.

Below we see what CrowdStrike reported: its ending ARR grew 24% to $5.25B and its net new ARR was $331M, up 47% YoY. Operating cash flow grew 44% YoY to $498M for the quarter, and the FCF margin was 29%. For SaaS companies that are in growth mode and are hard to value based on their earnings, investors turn to the Rule of 40, which requires that a company's revenue growth rate, combined with the FCF profit margin are equal to or greater than 40%. In CRWD's case, we have revenue growth of 23% and FCF margin of 29%, which add up to 51.

Now, we said that revenues were $1.31B. But the real story here is that $1.24B comes from subscriptions, which means that almost 95% of the company's top-line is recurring (and, almost consequently, growing). Moreover, we see that Q4 subscription revenue grew 23% compared to the FY result which was 21%. This means that CrowdStrike is indeed accelerating.

We have proof of this when we look at how the company's ARR grew over the past 13 quarters. At the end of FY23, it was $2.56B. It has more than doubled since then.

Let's now look at two other key metrics: gross and net retention rates for subscription ARR. We see that, throughout the year, gross retention was always at 97%, while net retention grew from 112% to 115%. Some may wonder how it is possible to have a retention rate above 100%, in particular, when we consider the net rate, which is affected by churn. Well, the math is easy to explain: if existing customers spend more compared to the previous year, the rate can go above 100%, even if gross retention shows a little and inevitable churn. After all, the net retention rate is calculated as the ratio between the current ARR from the customers and the ARR from the same customers one year ago. Think about how powerful this is. If CrowdStrike doesn't add a single customer, it can still expect revenue growth of around 15% annually. This is the "land-and-expand" model, which means that once a customer is landed, then CrowdStrike aims at expanding its bonds with the customer and cross-sells more services.

From what we see, the narrative of AI disruption doesn't find any confirmation as CRWD is growing, and this is why some analysts start pivoting back to the AI-as-a-tailwind narrative. I am on this side, too, as I agree when I see slides, such as the one below, which highlight that the agentic phase of AI could represent a multiplier moment for software utilization. We could see a transition from SaaS business models to consumption-based contracts. But I also think that subscriptions will win because companies will prefer to have their costs under control rather than paying variable fees depending on how they use agents. This is why we heard Mr. Kurt state during the earnings call that "AI is driving elevated demand for the Falcon platform and is a key accelerant for our business".

The guidance for FY27 that CRWD released is for total revenue growth of 22.6% and an AAR that will increase to $6.5B at the midpoint of the guidance, representing a 23.6% YoY growth. It means that the net ARR should be of around $1.24B. This is unambiguous: CrowdStrike expects to keep growing at a fast pace, if not even slightly faster than today. The non-GAAP operating income is expected to be around $1.44B, which means that CrowdStrike is guiding for an operating margin of approximately 24.4%. There is no guidance for this, but with this operating margin, an FCF margin of 30+% is feasible. We also know that the company targets a 15% ARR CAGR through 2036, which will mean that it will reach $20B in ARR.

Given these numbers, we can run some calculations. I think, in fact, that the earnings call confirmed that CrowdStrike is a solid and fast-growing business. So, it all comes down to the valuation. When I look at CrowdStrike's capital structure and calculate its WACC, I end up with 8.62% (risk-free rate: 4.07%, Damodaran's ERP: 4.32%, beta: 1.087). If I use this as the discount rate of CrowdStrike's FCF from FY27 through FY33, I end up with $16.05B. The terminal FCF will be $5B, and if we assume that the perpetual growth rate will be 3%, the terminal value of this FCF is $89B, whose present value is $50B. This means that we have an EV of $66B, and an equity value of $70.5B, and an intrinsic value of $280 per share.

We can then model our analysis based on bearish or bullish scenarios. If we expect that the terminal FCF margin will move up to around 43%, we could end up with an intrinsic value of $360. If we assume an even more bullish hypothesis - a 47% margin - we end up with an intrinsic value of $490.

CrowdStrike currently trades just above $400. Based on this analysis, it means that the stock is priced for a bullish scenario, although it is not in the priced-for-perfection category. In this situation, I don't think CrowdStrike is a complete hold, but neither do I think it is an extremely convincing buy. I see some upside, but I think it will be mainly narrative-driven in case we shift from AI-as-a-threat to AI-as-a-booster. But I also have to acknowledge that the valuation is somewhat steep, although it has compressed from its highs.

I think the odds of seeing CrowdStrike stock perform better as the year unwinds are increasing, and this is why I have decided to end with a buy rating. But I also want to disclose that I won't be buying the stock at this price because the margin of safety still seems to me to be a bit thin. So, take my buy as a "buy with caution", but take it also as a rating that wants to challenge the dominant narrative around software disruption that has recently rattled the market.

data.title: Corn: Runner Up In Oil Spike
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Recent geopolitical tensions and inflation concerns have ushered in a commodity boom, particularly in metals and energy. Grains, however, have remained relatively muted. I believe grains will soon follow suit, with corn as the primary beneficiary. In this article, I rate C_1:COM and CORN a STRONG BUY due to corn’s mechanical linkages to oil, stronger US corn exports, and increased likelihood of extreme weather events.

Catalyst #1: Mechanical Linkages to Oil

Recent conflicts in the Middle East have caused crude oil and gasoline prices to melt up 17.23% and 11.48%, respectively, since the US-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28. Corn stands to benefit from this.

In the US, 45% of all corn production goes into producing ethanol. Under the Renewable Fuel Standard mandate in the US, this ethanol is then blended into US gasoline. Therefore, if gasoline prices rise, ethanol becomes more economically attractive as a blending component for gasoline. This increases demand for corn feedstock, which increases corn prices.

Corn is special in that no other grain has an energy conversion pathway of comparable scale. Wheat has none. The closest would be soybeans, but soybean conversion to biodiesel represents a much smaller share of total soybean demand (~at 8.8%) than ethanol does for corn.

Historically, corn moved from $3.50/bu to $7.65/bu during the commodities melt-up in 2007-2008 and also from $1.28/bu to $4.00/bu in the months leading up to the oil crisis in 1973, which, interestingly, also originated from Iran. If history rhymes, we could see corn go from where it is now, $4.46/bu, to anywhere in between $9.72/bu and $13.94/bu.

Catalyst #2: Stronger US Corn Exports, Trend Unlikely To Reverse

According to WASDE, the US harvested a record 17.02 billion bushels of corn in 2025/26. However, they slashed the projected ending stocks to 2.127 billion bushels, down from 2.227 billion bushels. This is largely due to export demand hitting a record 3.3 billion bushels, a 29.4% increase from last year.

One of the main reasons for the increase in export demand is disruptions in trade flows due to geopolitical conflicts. For instance, Russia and Ukraine account for ~25% of world corn and wheat exports, a significant part of which flows through the Black Sea. However, the ongoing Russo-Ukrainian war has caused the Black Sea to become an unreliable trade route. It is unsurprising, then, that global importers have started to redirect more of their demand towards the US Gulf.

Since there is no clear end in sight for the Russo-Ukrainian war nor for the many other conflicts that have recently transpired in the Middle East, I do not expect this trend to reverse anytime soon. It is likely that the US increasingly becomes the default supplier of corn for Western-aligned importers, which is bullish for US corn.

Catalyst #3: Weather Wildcard

The increasing frequency of extreme weather events across the world is also bullish for corn prices. For instance, Argentina is currently in a severe drought, with the Rosario Grain Exchange placing corn in a “rain urgency” phase. Russia’s Volga region experienced crop winterkill in early January 2026 as insufficient snow caused dormant crops to die in sub-zero temperatures. Winter wheat ratings in Nebraska, US, collapsed from 54% good-to-excellent at the start of the year to only 24% by February 2026.

The World Meteorological Organization suggests that the global weather pattern is currently transitioning from La Niña to ENSO neutral, which is making an El Niño more likely in H2 2026. Historically, transitions to El Niño produced negative weather conditions for Northern Hemisphere growing seasons, as evidenced by the 1982-1983, 1997-1998, 2014-2016, and 2023-2024 seasons. Research suggests that climate change is also causing El Niño to occur more regularly, at intervals of 2 to 5 years instead of 2 to 7 years.

Risk #1: Oversupply

A main risk to corn prices is a global oversupply of corn. Brazil continues to produce record levels of corn (141.1 million tons in 2024/25 and projected to produce 138.9 million tons in 2025/26). The US keeps producing record highs themselves (17.02 billion bushels in 2025/26). Global corn stocks are currently sitting at 289.0 million tons, down 1.8% from the previous year. Although over the past 3 years, corn stocks have been in a multi-year downtrend, agricultural productivity is increasing due to technological advancements. If this trend reverses and corn supply grows, it could result in a supply glut and put downward pressure on prices.

Risk #2: Recession / Dollar Strength

A US recession, if it materializes, will be bearish for corn. The price transmission mechanism operates through several channels. First, during recessions, meat demand falls, which reduces feed demand. Second, gasoline/energy demand falls, which reduces ethanol demand. Third, the dollar likely gets a safe haven bid, which causes corn to become more expensive for global importers. The three channels tend to work violently and quickly.

It is important to note that historically, corn and other commodities—including oil—outperformed in the periods leading up to the end of business cycle slowdowns but fell hard thereafter. This was true particularly for long-term business cycles, e.g., in 1973, 1981, and 2008. For instance, in 2008, corn fell from its highs of $7.65 to $3.05 in just five months.

Therefore, investors should monitor US macro and manage their positions appropriately.

Investing in Corn: Corn Futures vs. The Teucrium Corn Fund ETF

Investors seeking to invest in corn can choose from two investment vehicles: corn futures (C_1:COM) or The Teucrium Corn Fund ETF (CORN).

Futures offer leverage but will require a futures account, margin management, and manual contract rolls. Profit and loss will also be calculated on a mark-to-market basis.

CORN is an ETF that holds a portfolio of corn futures contracts, handling rolls automatically and allowing investors to buy and sell their shares like any other ETFs. Unlike futures, CORN has no built-in leverage and will generally provide a 1:1 price exposure to corn (e.g., if corn prices increase by 10%, so will the price of the ETF).

Conclusion

In conclusion, corn offers an asymmetric risk-reward at current prices. The bull case is supported by three catalysts: a) corn’s mechanical oil-ethanol linkage in the context of soaring crude oil prices, b) record US exports driven by geopolitical supply disruptions, and c) increasing frequency of extreme weather events. However, investors should still exercise caution and monitor key risks, which include concerns of oversupply and a potential US/global recession.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Q4 Could Be A Turning Point
data.description:
ET Stock: Topline Growth Amid Mixed Q4

I last analyzed Energy Transfer LP Common Units stock (ET) on Jan 27 with an article titled “Energy Transfer: Why Super Investors Like It At $17.” The article focused on the latest disclosures filed by professional fund managers and concluded with a hold rating for ET stock. Since then, there have been a few new developments worth noting. In this article, I will focus on the top 3 on my list: the updates provided in its FQ4 2025 earnings report (ER), its latest dividend declaration, and also the changes in oil prices due to the evolving geopolitical conflicts, especially those involving Iran. These developments have led me to see the potential of 2025 Q4 as a turning point for the stock. Despite the various uncertainties and growth pressures facing the company, I see some encouraging signs that help to counterbalance the risks and make the stock a solid hold.

The first encouraging sign is the growth of topline reported in Q4 2025. To wit, the chart below summarizes the financial performance of Energy Transfer in recent quarters. As seen, the company has been facing revenue declines in recent quarters and often missed both consensus estimates on both lines. In 2025 alone, ET reported three consecutive quarters of declining revenue on a YOY basis: a -2.82% drop in FQ1 2025, a -7.17% drop in FQ2, and a -3.94% dip in FQ3. However, in Q4, the company reported revenue of $25.32B, marking a sharp 29.57% YOY increase that beat expectations by $1.28B.

ET Stock: Dividend Growth In Focus

The picture is not all rosy, though. Earnings still underperformed, with EPS dialing in at $0.25, compared to $0.29 a year ago, and falling below consensus by a large gap of $0.11. In tandem, the latest dividend declaration continues to show growth pressure. More specifically, the table below displays the dividend declarations from ET in recent quarters. On the positive side, the company kept maintaining its remarkable dividend growth trajectory, increasing its quarterly distribution from $0.3250 in February 2025 to $0.335 for the February 2026 payout ex-div date on Feb 6, 2026. With this new declaration, the stock’s current FWD yield stands at 7.1% at the price of this writing, quite attractive to dividend investors.

However, on the negative side, the new payout marks a YOY +3.07% increase and continues to show growth pressure. To better contextualize things, the table below outlines the dividend growth metrics for Energy Transfer and the overall sector in the past few years. As seen, ET’s growth rates have lagged behind sector medians in the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods. In the past 3 years, ET’s dividend growth averaged 9.83% CAGR, -8.15% below the sector’s 7.69% median rate. The 5Y CAGR growth rates lagged even more (by more than -21%). And its latest dividend growth rate of 3.07% is a far cry from its 3-year average CAGR.

ET Stock: Correlation With Oil Prices

Moving on to the changes in oil prices. The surging oil prices due to geopolitical conflicts do not benefit Energy Transfer directly because the partnership’s revenue is largely insulated from the underlying value of the commodities it transports. The vast majority of its segment margins are fee-based, meaning ET earns a fixed dollar amount per barrel or million cubic feet (Mcf) regardless of the spot oil price.

As a reflection, the following figure displays the correlation of ET and OXY’s cash from operations (CFO) with respect to the Brent Crude Oil Spot price in the past. I picked OXY in this comparison as OXY is a major oil producer and derives most of its profile from producing and selling oil; thus, its profit is more directly tied to oil prices. As seen in the top panel of the chart, ET’s CFO shows a lack of correlation with an average coefficient of essentially zero (0.094 to be exact) in the long term. In comparison, the correlation coefficient for OXY was much stronger, with a 10-year average of 0.573. Also note that OXY’s correlation has never dipped below zero in the past decade on a quarterly basis.

High oil prices can benefit Energy Transfer indirectly by incentivizing upstream producers to increase drilling activity, which ultimately drives the record volumes necessary to fill ET’s vast infrastructure. When oil prices remain elevated, producers in the Permian Basin and other key regions ramp up production to capture higher margins, leading to a direct surge in the demand for gathering, processing, and transportation services.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of valuation, ET is currently priced at a P/E ratio that is attractive relative to the sector but not relative to its own historical norms. As shown in the chart below, the stock currently holds an overall valuation grade of B+, reflecting these mixed valuation dynamics. For example, the company's FWD P/E ratio on a non-GAAP basis stands at 12.64 now, representing a significant -23.25% discount compared to the sector median of 16.48. However, despite being cheaper than its peers, this multiple represents an equally large premium to its own history. The FWD P/E ratio is 26.2% higher than its own 5-year average of 10.02x. Let me close by echoing a point I made earlier about its 7.1% FWD yield with the latest dividend announcement. As attractive as it is in absolute terms, this current dividend yield is actually slightly below the stock’s long-term average of 7.57%, as you can see from the following screenshot, serving as another sign of its valuation premium.

To conclude, this reexamination of ET was largely motivated by 3 changes since my last writing: its FQ4 2025 ER, its latest dividend declaration, and the changes in oil prices due to the conflicts involving Iran. Overall, I kept seeing a mixed picture with both positives and negatives. I see large odds for growth—both in terms of EPS and dividends—to be tempered in the next few years with the uncertainties of its capital projects, leverage, and future CAPEX investments (all elaborated in my last article). However, Q4 does reveal a few positives (in particular the topline growth and potential for more favorable oil prices) to offset these issues and thus maintain a well-balanced return/risk profile overall.

data.title: Kilroy Realty's Challenges Are Priced In (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Kilroy Realty Corporation (KRC) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 10% of their value. While it pays a large dividend, concerns continue to mount about office demand and the potential for declining occupancy. I last covered shares of Kilroy in July, rating the stock a “Sell” given concerns about its debt load, and since then, shares have dropped 17%, justifying my caution. With updated financials and with shares having dropped below my $33 price target, now is a good time to revisit Kilroy.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Kilroy Realty generated $0.97 of funds from operation (“FFO”), which was a penny below consensus as revenue declined by 5% to $272 million. For the full year, it generated $4.20 of FFO, down about 8.5% from 2024 levels. While revenue was down $14 million, property expenses were up 2% to $65 million. A challenge for the company is that the cost to operate an office building is fairly constant whether a building is 50% or 100% occupied. Declining occupancy is likely to pressure margins going forward. As a reminder, Kilroy primarily operates in legacy West Coast markets, though it also has a presence in Austin.

Occupancy was 81.6% in Q4, which was up 60 bps sequentially as it was able to pull forward start dates on previously leased space. Unfortunately, I expect this improvement in occupancy to be short-lived. It is also worth noting that while up sequentially, occupancy was down 220 bps from last year. Over the past year, we have seen a meaningful weakening in San Diego as occupancy fell by 550 bps to 83.7%; San Francisco also declined by 120 bps to 86.2%. LA was fairly stable at 75%, though this is a weak absolute level.

During the quarter, it leased 827k square feet of space, the best Q4 since before COVID. While it signed a substantial number of leases, new leases were 27% below prior levels on a cash basis, a strikingly large drop. This was due to two low leases, one replacing a bankrupt tenant and another on a single-tenant property to keep it occupied in the near term while Kilroy seeks out a long-term future for the building. Absent these, cash rents were still 2.6% lower.

Now, Kilroy has a fairly well-staggered pace of lease maturities, which limits the potential pace of decline in occupancy. About 8% of leases expire each of the next two years, and I generally expect KRC to release ~80% of maturities, limiting occupancy declines to about 1.5% per year. The backdrop will get more difficult in the 2030-2031 timeframe, but by then, development activity should help to offset pressures on the legacy portfolio to some degree.

One other challenge in renewing a lease is that office rents have deflated in many markets post-COVID. Kilroy estimates that in the LA and Bay Area markets, its rents are currently about 10% above market rates. In other markets, its rents are a bit below market levels, but overall, renewals are likely to be a headwind to cash flow and margins,

Given its West Coast footprint, Kilroy will be sensitive to the impact of AI. Frankly, the outcome here is highly uncertain. On the bright side, AI firms are leasing out more space as they build out their workforce. Their growth is a clear benefit to office demand. The question is how much of this growth cannibalizes other technology employment. In recent months, there has been extreme concern about the potential for AI to displace other software jobs. If AI creates fewer tech jobs than it replaces, it could be a net detractor from office demand. I view the outcome here as highly uncertain but am cautious that AI may prove a net headwind to demand and employment trends over the next 3-5 years.

Its major development project, Kilroy Oyster Point Phase 2 (KOP 2), is making progress, securing 316,000 sq. ft. of leases. This project now has a 44% lease rate. This project is focused on the life science sector and is now part of KRC’s stabilized portfolio. Just 3% of the footprint is occupied, and converting its leased space to occupied space quickly during 2026 is essential, as KRC generally only starts collecting revenue when the space is ready to be occupied. I expect occupancy to reach ~15% by year-end.

In Q4, it entered into an agreement to sell two properties for $210 million while closing a previously announced transaction for $61 million. Against these sales, it added a life science building in San Diego for $192 million. Kilroy has been fairly active in recycling capital and raising cash to support ongoing development work. For the full year, it sold $465 million of properties and acquired $395 million of assets. This is likely to continue, and KRC expects to complete $325 million of asset sales in 2026.

These sales are important because Kilroy has $4.6 billion of debt and $180 million of cash. That leaves it with an elevated 7.1x debt/EBITDA leverage, which is higher than I like to see, as I view ~6x as a better place to operate. Given the losses KOP 2 will generate this year, its leverage ratio will likely worsen on a stand-alone basis, which is why divestitures are essential to maintaining balance sheet sustainability. On the bright side, its debt is well-laddered, limiting near-term refinancing risk.

As you can see, though, its debt is relatively low-cost, as KRC borrowed wisely when rates were low. As it refinances this debt, we will likely see it roll over maturities at higher yields, which is likely to be a ~$0.05 headwind to annual FFO at least through 2028. Now, shares offer a 7.2% dividend yield. It retains about $130 million of FFO after its dividend, though I view KRC as having about $70 million of annual maintenance cap-ex needs. Still, this provides ~$60 million of retained cash flow. As such, I view the dividend as secure at least through 2027. Still, this elevated debt level and limited retained cash flow are why KRC has been active in disposing of assets to support development and acquisition activities.

Looking ahead, Kilroy provided guidance for 2026 that is unlikely to ease fears its business is a “melting ice cube.” It expects $3.25-$3.45 of FFO, which assumes a modest decline in same-property NOI of -1.5% to 0%, as occupancy will average just 76%-78%. Excluding KOP 2, occupancy will be 80%-81.5%, implying a tick down from Q4 levels. While management has highlighted an improvement in the leasing environment, it still is not sufficiently strong to offset the pace of lease maturities. Results also will be weighed down by a ~$25 million drag from KOP 2 and Flower Mart as these projects scale up. Their contribution should improve in 2027, though they will remain negative.

Frankly, the magnitude of the FFO decline guided to in 2026 was worse than I expected, reflecting the meaningful losses on KOP 2 and two adverse lease developments in Q4. Given its biggest lease for KOP 2 is not slated to occupy the site until late 2027, losses will continue here for some time, though at a lessening pace. Even for ex-KOP 2, FFO is likely to be below $3.50, or about $2.90-$3.00 after maintenance.

I believe KRC should trade no more than 10x FFO after maintenance, given we are likely to see a ~2% long-term decline as leases roll over and occupancy steadily falls. That points to a fair value in the $29-$30 area. This is a bit below where shares are today, but with its dividend, the total return opportunity is ~0%. This is not attractive, and I definitely would not buy KRC. However, I do not see sufficient downside to remain a seller. As such, I am moving shares to a “Hold.” On rallies past $33, I would exit KRC, but I would want shares to drop to the mid-$20s before buying, given secular pressures in the office sector.

data.title: Why China Is Less Vulnerable To The Strait Of Hormuz Than You Might Think
data.description:
China Needs The War In Iran To End Immediately... Right?

China is the world’s largest oil importer, and 50% of its imports come from Gulf countries through the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial chokepoint responsible for 25% of the world's maritime oil transportation. China is by far the country that benefits the most from it; in fact, 37% of that oil is directed toward it.

In light of this data, the situation seems pretty clear: China is doomed along with its investors. The country desperately relies on the Strait of Hormuz to sustain its huge and growing energy demand.

To give you some perspective, the Chinese primary energy consumption is more than that of the US and EU combined (almost 1/3 of the world's consumption). And it is growing rapidly. Therefore, blocking overnight 50% of the oil imports seems a severe blow for the Chinese economy, right? Well, not exactly. The situation is much more complex.

The aforementioned numbers are correct, but I purposely described just one part of the whole story, something that I saw in many analyses over the last days. In this article I am going to shed some light on China’s energy consumption and why it is less vulnerable to the Strait of Hormuz than you might think.

Numbers Must Be Contextualized, Always

First of all, China is the world’s largest oil importer, but that doesn’t imply that oil imports account for the majority of its energy consumption. The main reason why it holds this title stems from its huge manufacturing industry and a population of more than 1 billion.

In fact, oil's share of total energy consumption is ~ 18% (~ 27% including gas). Not a small number, but not even that concerning. Anyway, that ~ ~18% is the starting point that will lead us to understand the consequences of a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz in relation to China’s energy consumption.

Of that 18%, we have to exclude the domestic production, about 30% of the oil consumption. Therefore, oil imports have a weight of ~ 12% on total energy consumption. However, not all oil imports come through the Strait of Hormuz, just ~ 50% of them. And this last calculation brings us to the number that we were looking for: ~ ~6% (over 5 million daily barrels). Including gas imports wouldn’t change the situation that much; in fact, we would still be talking about a number that is less than 10%.

Overall, the fact that China relies so much on coal (52% of energy consumption) is a strong hedge against an oil supply shock in the Middle East. Iran depends on oil exports to China (87% of total exports), but not the other way around. First, because China has multiple partners to deal with (Russia above all), and second, China’s energy consumption is largely skewed toward coal and renewable energies; the latter have experienced a strong improvement over the last decade, and the current weight is comparable to that of oil consumption.

In other words, China relies on coal to sustain its huge manufacturing industry, and it is investing significantly in renewable sources for all the other scopes. Of course, to avoid misunderstanding, China is not yet independent from oil; otherwise, it wouldn’t take up 18% of the total consumption share. Its primary use is for logistics/transportation; after all, you can’t fill your car with coal, and the same goes for air and truck freight. However, we can objectively affirm that China is doing a great job to become less reliant on oil (we can’t say the same in terms of environmental pollution): it has evolved into the world’s largest EV market, and 50% of new cars sold are EVs. Electric airplanes are still utopian, but electric cars and trucks are already part of the present.

Final Thoughts

Overall, believing that China will suffer greatly from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a rather superficial statement; we have to go deeper. Only 6% of energy consumption depends on oil imports coming from the Strait of Hormuz; that’s because China has always preferred other types of energy sources, coal and renewables above all. For example, the US is in a totally different condition: oil and gas combined amount to 72% of total energy consumption.

If China were in a situation comparable to that of the US, the Strait of Hormuz closure would have been a very serious problem (the US is a net exporter of fossil fuels, unlike China), but this is not the case. And just for information, the Strait of Hormuz is not even the most important strait for China: the Strait of Malacca is way more strategic, both in terms of oil imports and trade volume.

All this doesn’t mean that China is immune to persistent oil shocks, but it is not in such a terrible situation considering that it is the world’s largest oil importer. We have to put this statement in context with all the other energy sources and the size of the country.

Furthermore, China is a country that tends to be careful in depending too much on the others, especially when it comes to energy: they know that a country based on the manufacturing industry is nothing if it can’t power its plants (unfortunately, Germany realized this too late).

It is not a coincidence that China has bet so much on renewables over the last few years and has started to accumulate a huge amount of onshore crude inventories: a record high of 1.20 billion barrels in early January. Considering that China typically imports 5 million oil barrels on a daily basis coming from the Strait of Hormuz, if closed, it would take 240 days (34 weeks) to run out of all the oil inventories. According to President Trump, the war should last 4-5 weeks, so it is well below the Chinese oil inventory capacity.

What if the war lasts more than 34 weeks? That’s a major concern, but not only for China, for all the world. If anything, China is less of a problem compared to the European Union and Japan, both oil-dependent economies that don’t produce it. Oil takes up 37% of Japan’s energy consumption, and 91% of it comes from Gulf countries. Basically, a third of the country’s energy consumption is threatened by the US-Iran war, and it doesn’t surprise me that the Japanese stock market has suffered greatly since 27 February. China has many more levers to pull before getting severely hit by this war, and for now it is still well covered. In a few months things can change, but that will be everybody’s problem.

data.title: Target: Sales Remain Weak, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Target Corporation (TGT) released its Q4 results in the after-market hours, and my initial read was that the sales side of the ledger reflects much of the same as in past quarters. That is, for the 13th straight quarter, the retailer reported flat or declining sales.

I’ve provided continuing coverage on TGT in the past, and my sentiment has been primarily neutral due to a combination of the sales picture as well as the multitude of headwinds TGT has faced over the years.

This includes its centerpiece in cultural political battles, its elevated exposure to shrink, and its unfavorable discretionary positioning in inflationary environments, which has created lopsided promotional drag relative to other off-price retailers.

Today, I remain neutral because I believe the retailer is very much in a “prove it” mode, and I believe that new CEO Michael Fiddelke has his work cut out for him.

Fiddelke does seem to have the right mindset, with stepped-up investments in the storefronts. These investments, however, could tap into TGT’s profitability, which has recently been on the mend.

I would need to see tangible and positive results from these investments, and until that happens, I would be content remaining neutral on the stock and its prospects.

TGT Stock Key Metrics

My neutral sentiment on TGT is echoed across-the-board from the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants and the broader analyst communities alike. The quants do grade TGT positively on most metrics, including valuation, but TGT is currently sitting on a capital “F” on growth, and I believe this is warranted.

Over the last three years, for example, TGT is sitting on a negative 1% CAGR for revenues. This compares to 5% for nearby peer Walmart (WMT).

Sentiment among the analyst communities is along the same lines.

In addition, from a price target perspective, analysts actually see some downside risk to the shares, with most valuing shares at the $115/share level, down about 5% from today’s $120/share price point. In my view, I believe TGT is valued appropriately at current trading levels.

TGT Stock Operating Performance

I’ve provided more negative remarks on TGT so far, but if there was one bright spot in the quarter, it was profitability.

While sales declined again, marking yet another quarter of pressure, TGT did manage to grow adjusted operating income and adjusted EPS in Q4 despite the revenue drop. That’s notable. It tells me that the management team is running a more disciplined ship on expenses, even while traffic and discretionary demand remain soft.

That said, gross margin was down modestly for the year, pressured by tariffs, though underlying margin trends were healthier than the headline suggests. Shrink, which has been a major issue for TGT, improved back to pre-pandemic levels, and that delivered a sizable benefit. Inventory levels were also described as appropriate, which reduces the risk of heavy markdowns.

It’s worth noting that margin stability in the face of declining sales isn’t the same as true operating momentum. The core issue remains growth. Management is guiding to roughly 2% sales growth this year, with comps turning slightly positive, but that follows a prolonged stretch of stagnation.

So while I’ll give TGT credit for protecting profitability and controlling costs, the operating story still hinges on whether these investments can actually drive sustained top-line reacceleration.

What Is The Outlook For TGT Stock?

If Q4 was about stabilizing profitability, 2026 is about betting big on a return to growth.

During the call, TGT’s CEO Fiddelke rolled out a +$6B turnaround effort. This includes roughly +$2B in incremental P&L and CAPEX investment layered on top of an already sizable capital base. The plan targets stores, staffing, merchandising resets, technology, and category reinvestment, particularly in home, apparel, and grocery.

Fiddelke was pretty blunt in his commentary, saying that TGT “lost its way.” That level of self-awareness is encouraging, and investors initially applauded the plan, sending shares higher immediately following the release.

But optimism doesn’t change the math, in my view. Comps fell 2.6% for the year, traffic declined, and this marks the 13th straight quarter of weak or falling sales. Against those facts, the team is guiding to just above flat net sales growth and EPS of $7.50 to $8.50, with performance weighted to the back half of the year.

In other words, growth is expected, but modestly.

The investments are significant, and the strategic refocus on differentiation and speed makes sense. However, TGT is competing against operational machines like Walmart, Costco (COST), and Amazon (AMZN), all of which are currently benefiting from positions of strength.

Is TGT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At this stage, I view TGT as a classic “show me” story. The TGT team deserves credit for stabilizing profitability in the face of prolonged sales pressure, improving shrink back to pre-pandemic levels, and maintaining tighter inventory discipline.

Those are meaningful operational wins. However, cost control alone cannot power a sustained rerating of the stock, in my view. The core issue in my mind remains growth. After 13 consecutive quarters of flat or declining sales, I would say that investors would be forgiven for demanding tangible evidence that traffic and comps can turn sustainably positive.

In fairness to TGT, new CEO Michael Fiddelke is taking decisive action with a +$6B turnaround effort, and the focus on stores, staffing, merchandising, and technology is strategically sound.

Still, I believe these investments come with risk and the potential for near-term margin pressure. Meanwhile, competition from scaled operators like Walmart, Costco, and Amazon remains intense.

With shares trading near consensus price targets and growth still uncertain, I believe a neutral view is warranted. Until we see consistent top-line reacceleration and clear evidence that the turnaround is gaining traction, I am comfortable maintaining a neutral stance and viewing TGT stock as fairly valued at current levels.

data.title: StoneCo: I Am Doubling Down To Secure A Double-Digit Buyback Yield
data.description:
My investment in StoneCo (STNE) stock back in November 2021 has not gone that well. After 4+ years, I am sitting on a meager gain of ~4% on my initial investment while the S&P 500 has rallied almost 50% during this time. As much as I would have enjoyed a better performance, I understand that this is a part of being a long-term oriented, contrarian investor with a focus on small-caps. My last bullish piece on StoneCo was published in December 2025 when I thought its credit portfolio growth was fundamentally different from what we saw in 2021.

On March 2, StoneCo reported Q4 2025 earnings. In reaction to the earnings print, STNE stock plunged yesterday, losing almost 20% of its value. I believe StoneCo's long-term shareholders are now used to this substantial volatility associated with earnings reports. However, a 20% drop in just one trading session requires scrutiny. Although I am comfortable with increased volatility, that doesn't mean I am okay with sideways trading that lasts several years, which has been the case with StoneCo over the past 4+ years.

After diving deep into the Q4 earnings print, I have come across a few red flags that need to be monitored closely. That said, StoneCo is offering a buyback yield of at least 10.6% today, with the possibility of a yield as high as 26% if StoneCo fully utilizes its buyback potential this year. Given my belief that StoneCo has a long runway to grow, I have decided to add to my long position today to make the most of this very attractive buyback yield.

Q4 Earnings Recap

For the fourth quarter of 2025, StoneCo reported revenue of R$3.7 billion, up 13% YoY. The adjusted gross profit in Q4 was R$1.7 billion, up 9% YoY. For the full year, adjusted gross profit grew 13.5% YoY to R$6.32 billion, which was below management's guidance for R$6.375 billion. On the back of an adjusted EPS of R$2.87 in Q4 (up 27% YoY), full-year adjusted EPS came to R$9.71, ahead of the guidance.

Exhibit 1: Profitability accelerated in 2025

StoneCo's MSMB TPV reached R$135 billion in Q4, a YoY increase of just 5%. This saturating growth was probably one of the biggest reasons behind the post-earnings sell-off on March 3. MSMB client base, however, grew 15% YoY to 4.7 million.

The banking business continued to gain traction in Q4. Retail deposits reached R$11.1 billion, up 27% YoY. This stellar growth came on the back of a 21% YoY growth in banking active clients to 3.7 million.

The credit portfolio, which I discussed extensively in my previous article, expanded at a healthy pace in Q4 as well.

Exhibit 2: Credit portfolio metrics

Overall, StoneCo had a decent fourth quarter. However, there were some red flags hidden in the report.

Brazil is a fast-growing fintech market. According to IMARC Group, the fintech industry in Brazil is expected to grow at a CAGR of ~15% between 2026 and 2034. StoneCo, unfortunately, experienced a notable saturation in growth in 2025. TPV growth of ~5% is lackluster to say the least. This growth saturation could be a sign of intensifying competition. The market does not like it when high-growth companies succumb to competitive pressures.

Then there is the lingering risk of a potential credit portfolio deterioration. As I discussed extensively in my previous article, StoneCo cannot afford to repeat the same mistakes it made in 2021. Although I am comfortable with the growth of its credit portfolio, the Q4 report highlights a deterioration in credit quality. NPLs for 15-90 days increased from 3.12% in Q3 to 4.43% in Q4. This was a notable increase. Commenting on this adverse development, management highlighted that this stemmed from payment delays from a certain segment of its clients.

Not to forget, this credit quality deterioration came on the back of a 135% YoY growth in StoneCo's credit portfolio. What this means is that StoneCo is aggressively growing its credit portfolio at a time when challenging macro conditions are already having an impact on the credit quality of its portfolio. This strategy gives us flashbacks of what happened back in 2021. The good news is that management seems to focus on scaling the credit portfolio cautiously going forward.

Understanding The 2027 Gross Profit Guidance Slash

StoneCo slashed its gross profit guidance for 2027, which was another major driver of the 20% stock decline on March 3. StoneCo now expects 2027 adjusted gross profit to range between R$7.2 billion and R$8.3 billion. Previously, the company had guided for a low-end adjusted gross profit of R$10.2 billion.

Exhibit 3: Updated guidance for 2026 and 2027

This massive guidance slash comes on the back of two factors.

  1. The impact of the Linx divestiture.
  2. Operational headwinds.

Any guidance cut coming from the divestiture of Linx is nothing to be concerned about as it represents a strategic decision by the company to focus on its core business. As confirmed by CFO Diego Salgado on the earnings call, the Linx divestment is expected to have a R$2 billion impact on adjusted gross profit in 2027.

Even after accounting for this, there is another R$1 billion impact that is unaccounted for (based on the difference between the updated and previous lower end of the guidance for adjusted gross profit). This difference can be explained by the impact of operational headwinds. Management now has a more cautious view on TPV growth amid the challenges faced by small businesses in Brazil. This is a major challenge for growth. In addition to this, StoneCo seems to be caught offguard because of its massive presence among physical retailers at a time when online-only stores are taking over the retail space in Brazil. While this is a positive long-term development, StoneCo may face growth challenges in the short run.

Buybacks Create A Price Floor; Time To Double Down

StoneCo has been aggressively deploying capital for shareholder distributions through buybacks. In 2025, the company returned R$3 billion to shareholders via buybacks. For 2026, management has approved an R$2 billion buyback program. This comes to ~$378 million based on today's BRL/USD exchange rate of 5.28. In addition to this authorized repurchase program, StoneCo plans to distribute the proceeds it raised by divesting Linx to shareholders as well. Based on company filings, we are looking at another R$3 billion distribution as a result, or $568 million. Combined, the total buyback potential for this year comes to $946 million. StoneCo may decide to return a portion of this capital through a different mechanism, such as a special dividend, but the logic remains the same for our calculations here.

Exhibit 4: Extraordinary capital distribution plans

A quick calculation of the buyback yield reveals how attractive STNE is today.

To be on the conservative side, let's assume that StoneCo will only complete its authorized buyback program this year. Total buybacks will come to $378 million in this case. At the current market price of $13.73, StoneCo can repurchase ~27.5 million shares with this. In other words, the company can retire almost 11% of its float with its authorized buyback program this year. In reality, total buybacks are likely to be much higher given that the company has pledged to distribute Linx sale proceeds of $568 million. If its buyback potential is maximized, StoneCo can retire ~27% of its float at today's prices by repurchasing 69 million shares. Even if StoneCo's earnings remain flat this year, we are looking at the possibility of a much higher EPS thanks to the impact of these buybacks.

Based on the current market capitalization of $3.6 billion, below are the calculated buyback yields for each of these scenarios.

  1. Buyback yield based on the authorized repurchase program for 2026: 10.5%
  2. Buyback yield based on the full buyback potential for 2026: 26.3%

Even in the base-case scenario, we are looking at a very attractive buyback yield of 10.5%. In reality, though, the buyback yield could be as high as 26.3%.

I find this buyback yield quite attractive given my belief that the long-term outlook remains promising for StoneCo. As I have discussed in detail in my previous articles, there are many reasons behind this thinking, including the company's transition into a full banking platform, the data advantage enjoyed by the company thanks to its merchant payments business, and the favorable long-term industry outlook for the fintech sector in Latin America.

Risks

As I pointed out in an earlier segment, StoneCo's credit portfolio growth needs to be monitored carefully. A meaningful deterioration of credit quality will be punished badly. Investors are already getting flashbacks of StoneCo's 2021 debacle.

Competitive threats also need to be monitored. At a time when StoneCo is transitioning to a full banking business model, the last thing we want to see is its payments business losing market share to competitors. Recent earnings suggest the presence of intense competition.

I am also keeping an eye on how StoneCo's leadership change will impact the company's long-term growth strategy.

Takeaway

StoneCo stock crashed after reporting Q4 2025 earnings. The market was spooked by the company's gross profit guidance miss as well as the slashing of its 2027 guidance. Although there are some red flags, I still believe the long-term outlook is bright due to several reasons. The massive buyback yield has incentivized me to double down on STNE stock to make the most of this crash.
